metadata.description: A curated knowledge base on cyber hygiene practices, threats, and source-grounded recommendations. The knowledge base supports a knowledge graph-grounded conversational agent and enables evaluation across validity, traceability, and stability.
curation_notes: Some threat mappings are initially inferred from Karayel et al.'s cyber hygiene categories and should later be supported by additional sources.
Page paragraph numbers are counted visually from the top-left of the page to the bottom-right of the page, regardless of section boundaries.

sources.title: Human factors in remote work: examining cyber hygiene practices
sources.notes: Primary source for cyber hygiene practices among remote workers and for the five cyber hygiene categories used as the initial taxonomy of the knowledge base.

sources.title: Cyber hygiene: The concept, its measure, and its initial tests
sources.notes: Original source for defining cyber hygiene as a concept, developing and validating the Cyber Hygiene Inventory, and identifying its five dimensions: storage/device hygiene, authentication/credential hygiene, social media hygiene, email/messaging hygiene, and transmission/browsing hygiene. Used as a primary source for the KB taxonomy and for source-listed cyber hygiene practices.

sources.title: An exploratory study of cyber hygiene behaviors and knowledge
sources.venue: Journal of Information Security and Applications
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed exploratory survey and literature review on end-user cyber hygiene knowledge and behavior. Used to support KUs on user behavior, actionable knowledge gaps, password hygiene, phishing-related behaviors, unsafe email behavior, social media oversharing, and differences between cybersecurity knowledge and protective behavior.

sources.title: Cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices of university students
sources.venue: Information Security Journal: A Global Perspective
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed study on cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices among university students. Used to strengthen KUs on knowledge-awareness-behavior relationships, student cyber hygiene, storage/device practices, transmission and browsing hygiene, authentication behavior, social media hygiene, email and messaging hygiene, and user behavior change.

sources.title: A Survey on Ransomware: Evolution, Taxonomy, and Defense Solutions
sources.notes: Comprehensive survey of ransomware evolution, taxonomy, attack phases, target platforms, infection vectors, defense research, detection, and recovery mechanisms across PCs/workstations, mobile devices, and IoT/CPS platforms.

sources.title: Analyzing and comparing the effectiveness of malware detection: A study of machine learning approaches
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed malware detection paper focused on comparing machine-learning approaches for malware detection and classification. Used selectively for broad malware context, malware detection limitations, and the distinction between signature-based and behavior-based detection approaches. Not used as the main authority for precise malware subtype definitions.

sources.title: Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling for Desktops and Laptops
sources.notes: Authoritative NIST technical guidance on malware incident prevention and handling for desktops and laptops. Used for malware definitions, malware forms, layered prevention, user awareness, vulnerability mitigation, threat mitigation tools, antivirus limitations, incident response, containment, eradication, and recovery. This is not a peer-reviewed journal article, but it is an official NIST Special Publication and is treated as an authoritative technical source.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: General cyber hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: General concepts and foundational definitions related to cyber hygiene.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Storage and device hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Security measures and behaviors related to the configuration settings of digital devices.
examples: Conducting virus scans on storage devices
Keeping virus protection and device software up to date

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Transmission hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors when connecting to unknown or insecure wireless networks.
examples: Checking the quality of SSL certificates
Checking the lock icon in the web browser

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Social media hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors during the general use of social media.
examples: Evaluating the authenticity of friend requests
Being aware of who you are friends with on social media
Preventing location information from leaking in posts

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Authentication and credential hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to the use of passwords and authentication.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Email and messaging hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to email usage and messaging.
examples: Checking the subject of an incoming email
Checking the sender's email domain name
Checking spelling and grammar of email requests

threats.name: Malware infection
threats.definition: A malware infection involves malicious code or software that compromises a device, application, operating system, or data. Malware may be covertly inserted with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim's data, applications, or operating system.
threats.support_notes: NIST SP 800-83 Rev. 1 is the primary source for the malware definition, describing malware, also known as malicious code, as a program covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim's data, applications, or operating system. Azeem et al. (2024) provide secondary support by describing malware as software used to monitor machine activity, obtain access to a computer system, or gather critical information. Cain et al. (2018) may support malware-related user behavior or phishing consequences in specific KUs, but is not used here as a primary malware definition source.

threats.name: Data loss
threats.definition: Data loss refers to losing access to important files or information, especially when malware or ransomware restricts, encrypts, deletes, or overwrites files. In the ransomware context, cryptographic ransomware can encrypt victim files and may delete or overwrite original files, restricting access until a ransom is paid.
threats.support_notes: Oz et al. (2022) directly support the ransomware-related data loss definition by explaining that cryptographic ransomware encrypts victim files and can delete or overwrite original files, restricting access until ransom payment. Karayel et al. (2025) support the related storage/device hygiene practices of backing up important files and secure data storage, but should be treated as practice support rather than the primary definition source for data loss.

threats.name: Ransomware
threats.definition: Ransomware is a subset of malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system and/or data until a ransom is paid.
threats.support_notes: Oz et al. (2022) directly define ransomware as malware that restricts access to a system or data until a ransom is paid. They also distinguish cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts victim files, from locker ransomware, which prevents access to the system.

threats.name: Unpatched software
threats.definition: Unpatched software refers to software or applications that have not received available security updates or patches, leaving known vulnerabilities unresolved. In the ransomware context, such vulnerabilities can be exploited by attackers, and delayed upgrades or security-related patches can contribute to successful infection.
threats.support_notes: Oz et al. (2022) directly support the vulnerability-risk framing by explaining that ransomware can exploit vulnerabilities in system software or applications and that delayed upgrades or security-related software patches can contribute to ransomware infection. Karayel et al. (2025) support the related cyber hygiene practice of keeping device software up to date, but should be treated as practice support rather than the main threat-definition source.

threats.name: Insecure connection
threats.definition: An insecure connection refers to risky use of unknown, public, unsecured, or insufficiently trusted network connections, or websites without SSL/TLS security indicators. Such connections are relevant to transmission and browsing hygiene, especially when users perform sensitive activities or enter personal information online.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. (2025) directly link transmission hygiene to unknown or insecure wireless networks and browsing hygiene to websites without SSL security certificates. Baraković and Baraković Husić (2023) support this threat context by discussing risks related to unknown or unsecured Wi-Fi networks, VPN use on public Wi-Fi, SSL certificate checks, browser lock checks, and student uncertainty about password-protected Wi-Fi safety and default Wi-Fi traffic encryption. Cain et al. (2018) should only be added if the KB contains a direct evidence object supporting unsecured Wi-Fi or insecure connection risk.

threats.name: Man-in-the-middle attack
threats.definition: A man-in-the-middle attack involves a third party intercepting, observing, or manipulating communication between two parties.
threats.support_notes: This threat is currently inferred from transmission hygiene practices related to SSL certificate checks, browser lock checks, unknown or insecure wireless networks, and public Wi-Fi use. The KB does not yet contain a direct source defining man-in-the-middle attacks. Do not present this threat as directly literature-supported until a source with a direct MITM definition is added.

threats.name: Social engineering
threats.definition: Social engineering is treated in this knowledge base as a user-facing threat context involving deceptive or manipulative attempts that can lead users to reveal information or perform unsafe actions, especially through email, messaging, phishing, or social media interactions.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. mention social engineering frauds in relation to email and messaging hygiene. Cain et al. support the connection between personal-information exposure and social engineering attacks such as spear phishing. However, the KB currently does not contain a dedicated source that directly defines social engineering, so this threat should not be treated as having a fully sourced standalone definition.

threats.name: Impersonation
threats.definition: Impersonation is treated in this knowledge base as a deception-related threat context where users may need to assess whether a social media request, email sender, or displayed sender identity is authentic.
threats.support_notes: Vishwanath et al. support the social-media side through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001, which lists assessing the authenticity of social media friend/information requests. Cain et al. support the email-identity side through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003, which lists mismatches between the displayed sender name and address in the From field as a warning sign. Karayel et al. support related email hygiene through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001, which includes checking the sender's email domain name. These evidence objects support impersonation as a threat context, but the KB does not yet contain a dedicated standalone definition source for impersonation.

threats.name: Oversharing personal information
threats.definition: Oversharing personal information refers to exposing personal or sensitive information through excessive or poorly controlled sharing, especially on social networking sites. This can include sharing private information, addresses, phone numbers, dates of birth, email addresses, location information, or passwords in ways that increase exposure to attacker interest or social engineering.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) directly support this threat through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-002, which identifies freely sharing passwords and private information over social networks as poor cyber hygiene; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-004, which states that personal information can be stolen when users post it on social networking sites; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-005, which states that personal information can be used in social engineering attacks such as spear phishing; and EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-008, which states that many users share too much personal information on social media and often do not check privacy settings. Vishwanath et al. (2020) support related social media hygiene practices through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-004 on managing privacy settings and EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-005 on preventing location information leaks in posts.

threats.name: Privacy exposure
threats.definition: Privacy exposure refers to the unintended or poorly controlled disclosure of personal information through unsafe online behavior, weak social media privacy practices, or information shared in posts. This can include exposure of names, dates of birth, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, or location information.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) directly support this threat through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-004, which states that personal information can be stolen when users post it on social networking sites; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-006 and EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-007, which report participant behavior involving real names, dates of birth, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, and privacy settings on social media; and EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-008, which states that many users share too much personal information on social media and often do not check privacy settings. Vishwanath et al. (2020) support related social media hygiene practices through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-004 on managing privacy settings and EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-005 on preventing location information leaks. Baraković and Baraković Husić (2023) support related social media hygiene practices through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001.

threats.name: Credential targeting
threats.definition: Credential targeting refers to attacker attempts to obtain, steal, or exploit user credentials or related user information, such as usernames, passwords, or other sensitive account information.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. support this threat through EVID-KARAYEL-AUTH-002, which states that user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers. Cain et al. support this threat through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-003, which states that attackers may seek to steal user information, and EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002, which states that phishing scams can result in the release of sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and credit card information.

threats.name: Credential theft
threats.definition: Credential theft is treated in this knowledge base as a threat context where attackers obtain or cause the disclosure of user credentials or related sensitive account information, such as usernames and passwords.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. support this threat through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-003, which states that attackers may seek to steal user information, and EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002, which states that phishing scams can result in the release of sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and credit card information. The specific label 'credential theft' is a knowledge-base mapping rather than a standalone formal definition from Cain et al.

threats.name: Brute-force credential attack
threats.definition: A brute-force credential attack is treated in this knowledge base as a credential threat related to password guessing, where weak passwords can make accounts more vulnerable to guessing or brute-force attacks.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. support this threat through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-002, which states that users are advised to select strong passwords to prevent guessing attacks. Baraković and Baraković Husić support the brute-force terminology through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-003, which states that weak passwords invited brute-force attack. The KB does not currently contain a standalone formal definition of brute-force attacks as repeated automated login attempts, so this threat remains partially supported rather than fully defined.

threats.name: Unauthorized access
threats.definition: Unauthorized access refers to access to an account, device, or system without legitimate permission. In this knowledge base, it is currently treated as an inferred threat context connected to weak authentication, default credentials, password reuse, and missing multifactor authentication.
threats.support_notes: This threat is inferred from authentication and credential hygiene practices such as changing default usernames and passwords, creating unique credentials, avoiding password reuse, and enabling multifactor authentication. The KB does not currently contain a direct evidence object defining unauthorized access as a standalone threat.

threats.name: Phishing
threats.definition: Phishing is treated in this knowledge base as a deceptive email, messaging, or computer-based threat that can lead users to disclose sensitive information, download malware, or interact with fraudulent requests that appear trustworthy.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. support phishing as an email and messaging hygiene threat through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002 and EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003. Cain et al. support phishing consequences and warning signs through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002 and EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003. NIST supports phishing-related defensive guidance through EVID-NIST-PHISHING-SOCIAL-ENGINEERING-001, which advises users not to reply to financial or personal information requests and to contact the real organization through legitimate channels. If a separate NIST phishing definition evidence object is added, this node can use that as the primary definition support.

threats.name: Email scam and e-fraud
threats.definition: Email scam and e-fraud are treated in this knowledge base as email or messaging-based fraud contexts where users may be deceived into unsafe actions or disclosure of information.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. support this threat context through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002, which links email and messaging hygiene to preventing phishing and e-fraud, and EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003, which links caution and awareness to preventing social engineering frauds and phishing attacks. The KB does not currently contain a standalone formal definition of email scam or e-fraud.

threats.name: User noncompliance with security practices
threats.definition: User noncompliance with security practices refers to a behavioral cybersecurity risk in which users do not follow recommended or required security practices, weakening cyber hygiene and increasing exposure to security incidents.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. support this threat through EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-001, which states that cybersecurity investments and measures alone are insufficient; EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-002, which states that human factors often constitute the weakest link; and EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-003, which identifies noncompliance with security practices as a human-factor concern.

threats.name: Insufficient organizational cybersecurity measures
threats.definition: Insufficient organizational cybersecurity measures refer to weak or inadequate organizational security measures that may be associated with poorer employee cyber hygiene practices, especially in remote work contexts.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. support this threat through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-MEASURES-001, which reports a significant correlation between company security measures and employees' cyber hygiene practices, and EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-MEASURES-002, which reports a statistically significant positive relationship between companies' cybersecurity measures and individual cyber hygiene behaviour. This should be interpreted as an association, not proof of strict causation.

threats.name: Web-based attack
threats.definition: A web-based attack is treated in this knowledge base as a malware-related threat context involving web-based malware, malicious websites, browser-based delivery, malicious web content, or attacker tools delivered through web activity.
threats.support_notes: NIST supports this threat through its malware forms and attacker-tools discussion, including web-based malware, phishing overlap with web-based malware, rootkits, malicious browser plug-ins, and attacker tools delivered through malware. Use the relevant NIST evidence object from KU-NIST-MALWARE-DEFINITION-FORMS-001, or create/check an evidence object such as EVID-NIST-WEB-BASED-MALWARE-001 before treating this source mapping as valid. Cain et al. should only be added if a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object exists that explicitly mentions web-based attacks as a cybercrime category.

threats.name: Malicious code
threats.definition: Malicious code refers to malware: a program covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of data, applications, or an operating system.
threats.support_notes: NIST directly supports this definition by defining malware, also known as malicious code, as a covertly inserted program intended to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability. Azeem et al. support related malware context through EVID-AZEEM-MALWARE-DEFINITION-001, which defines malware as software used to monitor machine activity, obtain access to a computer system, or gather critical information. Cain et al. should be omitted unless a specific EVID-CAIN-* object is used only to support that malicious code was mentioned as a cybercrime category.

threats.name: Botnet
threats.definition: A botnet is treated in this knowledge base as a malware-related threat involving compromised hosts or bots that can be controlled or used to attack other hosts or perform malicious activity.
threats.support_notes: NIST supports this threat through its attacker-tools discussion, where zombies, better known as bots, are described as backdoors installed on hosts to cause them to attack other hosts. Azeem et al. support botnet as a malware class through EVID-AZEEM-MALWARE-TYPES-001, which lists botnet among malware classes. Cain et al. should only be added if a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object exists that explicitly mentions botnets as a cybercrime category.

threats.name: Stolen device
threats.definition: Stolen device is treated in this knowledge base as a physical-device threat category that may be relevant to storage and device hygiene. The KB currently treats the exposure of systems, accounts, or stored information as a reasonable threat interpretation, not as a fully sourced standalone definition.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. may support this only if the KB contains a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object showing that stolen devices are mentioned as a cybercrime or attack category. The current KB should not present Cain et al. as providing a detailed definition of stolen-device risk or as directly supporting all consequences such as account or information exposure.

threats.name: Denial-of-service attack
threats.definition: Denial-of-service attack is treated in this knowledge base as an attack category involving disruption or prevention of normal access to a service, system, or network. The KB currently treats this as a threat-category label rather than a fully sourced standalone definition.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. may support this only if the KB contains a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object showing that denial-of-service attacks are mentioned as a cybercrime or attack category. Cain et al. should not be treated as providing a detailed denial-of-service definition. Add a direct technical source if this threat needs to support explanatory answers.

threats.name: Malicious insider
threats.definition: Malicious insider is treated in this knowledge base as an organizational threat category involving harmful or unauthorized actions by someone with insider access to an organization, system, or data. The KB currently treats this as a mentioned threat category rather than a fully sourced standalone definition.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. should only support this threat if the KB contains a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object showing that malicious insiders are mentioned as a cybercrime or attack category. Cain et al. should not be treated as providing a detailed definition of malicious insider threats.

threats.name: Infected USB drive
threats.definition: An infected USB drive is treated in this knowledge base as a removable storage threat in which using an infected USB drive can compromise a network or contribute to malware-related harm.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. support this threat if the KB contains a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object stating that using infected USB drives can compromise a network and can lead to problems such as malware download. Use that exact evidence object in KU-level evidence mappings. If that EVID object is missing, this threat should be downgraded to needs_direct_source.

threats.name: Keystroke logger
threats.definition: A keystroke logger is an attacker tool that monitors and records keyboard use, and may transfer the captured data to another host.
threats.support_notes: NIST supports this threat if the KB contains a concrete EVID-NIST-* object stating that keystroke loggers monitor and record keyboard use, and may transfer the captured data to another host. Use that exact evidence object in KU-level evidence mappings. If this evidence object is not yet present, add it from the NIST malware forms or attacker-tools section before relying on this threat as directly supported.

practices.name: Conduct virus scans on storage devices
practices.description: Users should conduct virus scans on storage devices, especially new USB drives or external hard drives, before use to reduce exposure to potentially malicious or infected files.
practices.support_notes: Karayel et al. support virus scans on storage devices through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Vishwanath et al. support scanning new USB or external storage devices through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Baraković and Baraković Husić support storage/device hygiene examples including virus scans through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. The infected-USB mitigation is especially supported by the USB/external-storage wording.

practices.name: Back up important files
practices.description: Users should back up important files to reduce the impact of data loss or malware-related file access loss, and backups can support ransomware recovery planning.
practices.support_notes: Karayel and Vishwanath directly list backing up important files through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-001 and EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-002. Cain's source-listed government guidance includes backing up data/files through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić list backups as storage/device hygiene through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Oz et al. support the ransomware recovery context through EVID-OZ-RANSOMWARE-RECOVERY-001 and EVID-OZ-RANSOMWARE-BACKUP-RECOVERY-001. Do not claim backups guarantee complete ransomware recovery.

practices.name: Keep protection and device software up to date
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus protection, security software, operating systems, applications, and device software up to date.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports keeping protection and device software up to date through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Cain supports updating security software, applications, operating systems, and software through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-002 and EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-003. Vishwanath supports checking OS/software/patch status and keeping virus protection updated through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-004 and EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-006. Baraković supports latest OS and updated virus protection/software through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Oz supports not delaying security-related patches in ransomware risk through EVID-OZ-RANSOMWARE-PATCHING-001.

practices.name: Check SSL certificate quality
practices.description: Users should check whether a website uses SSL or a valid SSL certificate before entering sensitive information or carrying out sensitive online activity.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports checking SSL certificate quality through EVID-KARAYEL-TRANSMISSION-001. Vishwanath supports checking SSL certificate quality for online financial transactions through EVID-VISHWANATH-TRANSMISSION-001. Baraković and Baraković Husić list checking SSL certificate quality as good transmission/browsing hygiene through EVID-BARAKOVIC-TRANSMISSION-002.

practices.name: Check browser lock icon
practices.description: Users should check for the browser lock icon or similar browser security indicators to assess whether a website connection appears secured before sensitive activity.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports checking the browser lock icon through EVID-KARAYEL-TRANSMISSION-001. Vishwanath supports checking the lock icon/SSL indicator to see whether a website uses encryption through EVID-VISHWANATH-TRANSMISSION-002. Baraković and Baraković Husić list checking the browser lock icon as good transmission/browsing hygiene through EVID-BARAKOVIC-TRANSMISSION-002. Do not claim the lock icon guarantees safety.

practices.name: Evaluate authenticity of friend requests
practices.description: Users should evaluate whether social media friend or information requests are authentic before accepting or responding to them.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports assessing the authenticity of social media friend/information requests through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. Baraković and Baraković Husić list the same social-media hygiene practice through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. The impersonation mitigation is direct; the social-engineering mitigation is a broader deception-related mapping.

practices.name: Manage privacy settings
practices.description: Users should manage social media privacy settings to better control who can view their personal information.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports managing social media privacy settings through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-004. Baraković and Baraković Husić include managing privacy settings among social-media hygiene examples through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. Cain supports the risk context through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-008, which states that many users share too much personal information and often do not check privacy settings.

practices.name: Change default usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should change default usernames and passwords to reduce exposure from unchanged default credentials.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports changing default usernames and passwords through EVID-KARAYEL-AUTH-001. Vishwanath supports changing default usernames and default passwords through EVID-VISHWANATH-AUTH-001 and EVID-VISHWANATH-AUTH-002. Baraković and Baraković Husić describe authentication and credential hygiene as including changing default usernames and passwords through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-001. The unauthorized-access mitigation remains partly inferred because the THR itself lacks a direct definition source.

practices.name: Create complex usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should create complex usernames and passwords, avoiding easily guessed choices such as personal information or dictionary words.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports creating complex usernames and passwords through EVID-KARAYEL-AUTH-001. Cain supports strong passwords for guessing attacks and avoiding personal information/dictionary words through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-002 and EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić support the brute-force context through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-003. Do not claim strong passwords alone protect against phishing or keylogging.

practices.name: Enable multifactor authentication
practices.description: Users should enable multifactor authentication to add an additional layer of account security beyond usernames and passwords.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports enabling multifactor authentication through EVID-KARAYEL-AUTH-001. Vishwanath supports enabling two-factor or multifactor authentication through EVID-VISHWANATH-AUTH-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić describe MFA as enhancing security by requiring more than one authentication factor through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-004. The unauthorized-access mitigation is reasonable but partly inferred.

practices.name: Check incoming email subjects
practices.description: Users should inspect the subject of incoming emails and be cautious of suspicious, unexpected, or urgency-based requests that may indicate phishing or e-fraud.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports checking the subject of incoming emails through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Cain supports urgency and other phishing warning signs through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003. The social-engineering mitigation is a broader deception-related mapping.

practices.name: Check sender email domain
practices.description: Users should check the sender's email domain name before trusting an email request.
practices.support_notes: Karayel and Vishwanath support checking sender email domains through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001 and EVID-VISHWANATH-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002. Baraković and Baraković Husić also list checking sender domain names through EVID-BARAKOVIC-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Cain supports related sender-identity warning signs through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003, which mentions mismatched sender name/address.

practices.name: Check incoming email headers
practices.description: Users should check an incoming email's header as part of email and messaging hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports checking incoming email headers through EVID-VISHWANATH-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Baraković and Baraković Husić also list checking incoming email headers through EVID-BARAKOVIC-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Mitigation links to phishing and impersonation are reasonable email-authenticity mappings.

practices.name: Check spelling and grammar of email requests
practices.description: Users should inspect spelling and grammar in email requests as possible warning signs of suspicious or fraudulent messages.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports checking spelling and grammar through EVID-KARAYEL-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Vishwanath supports checking grammatical or typographical errors through EVID-VISHWANATH-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić list the same type of email/messaging hygiene practice through EVID-BARAKOVIC-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001. Cain supports spelling/grammar issues as phishing warning signs through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003.

practices.name: Address individual user behavior
practices.description: Cybersecurity guidance should address individual user behavior and human factors in addition to technical security measures.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports the need to address human factors because technical measures alone are insufficient, human factors are a weak link, and noncompliance is relevant through EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-001, EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-002, and EVID-KARAYEL-HUMAN-FACTORS-003. Cain supports the user-behavior framing through EVID-CAIN-HUMAN-FACTORS-001 and EVID-CAIN-HUMAN-FACTORS-002.

practices.name: Use strong passwords
practices.description: Users should use strong passwords as part of routine cyber hygiene, avoiding easily guessed passwords such as those based on personal information or dictionary words.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports strong passwords as routine cyber hygiene through EVID-KARAYEL-ROUTINE-PRACTICES-001. Cain supports strong passwords for preventing guessing attacks and avoiding personal information/dictionary words through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-002 and EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić support the weak-password/brute-force context through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-003.

practices.name: Change passwords regularly
practices.description: Users should change passwords regularly as part of maintaining authentication and credential hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports changing passwords regularly through EVID-KARAYEL-ROUTINE-PRACTICES-001. Cain supports changing passwords often through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-004. The mitigation is mapped to credential targeting rather than credential theft because regular password changes do not directly prevent phishing or keylogging.

practices.name: Avoid password reuse
practices.description: Users should avoid reusing the same password across multiple accounts to reduce credential-related exposure.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports avoiding password reuse through EVID-KARAYEL-ROUTINE-PRACTICES-001. Cain supports not using passwords for multiple accounts through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-004. Baraković and Baraković Husić identify password reuse as lax security behavior through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-002. The unauthorized-access mitigation is reasonable but partly inferred.

practices.name: Update antivirus software
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus or virus protection software up to date as part of storage and device hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports keeping virus protection and antivirus software up to date through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-001 and EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-002. Vishwanath supports keeping virus protection updated through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-006. Cain supports updating security software and reports antivirus-update behavior through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-002 and EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-005. Baraković and Baraković Husić support keeping virus protection/software updated through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001.

practices.name: Securely store information
practices.description: Users should securely store information, including physical and cloud-based data, as part of storage and device hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports secure data storage, including physical and cloud-based data, through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-002, and secure online information storage through EVID-KARAYEL-ROUTINE-PRACTICES-001. The privacy-exposure mitigation is a reasonable mapping when stored information is personal or sensitive.

practices.name: Conduct appropriate security scans
practices.description: Users should conduct regular virus or security scans, especially on storage devices such as new USB drives or external storage devices.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports virus/security scans through EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-001 and EVID-KARAYEL-STORAGE-DEVICE-002. Vishwanath supports scanning new USB/external storage through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Cain supports antivirus scans through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-003 and EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-005. Baraković and Baraković Husić support virus scans and scanning downloaded files/used devices through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001 and EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-BEHAVIOR-002.

practices.name: Strengthen organizational cybersecurity measures
practices.description: Organizations should strengthen cybersecurity measures that support employees' cyber hygiene practices.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports this practice through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-MEASURES-001 and EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-MEASURES-002, which report an association between company cybersecurity measures and employee cyber hygiene practices, and through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-004, which recommends strengthening cybersecurity measures in remote work systems.

practices.name: Provide cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should educate and train employees on cyber hygiene to support improved cyber hygiene behavior and awareness.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports educating/training employees through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-004, comprehensive training/awareness through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-005, and incident management training/educational materials through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-006.

practices.name: Provide tailored cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should tailor cyber hygiene improvement initiatives and training to employees' characteristics, such as educational level, age, or comprehension needs.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports tailoring improvement initiatives to educational levels through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-002 and focused/simplified training for older workers through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-003. This should be framed as tailoring by educational level, age, or comprehension needs, not as a universal claim that all users need different training.

practices.name: Develop cybersecurity policies and procedures
practices.description: Organizations should develop cybersecurity policies and procedures that support remote work systems and foster a cyber hygiene culture.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports cybersecurity policies for remote work systems through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-004, policies/procedures for fostering cyber hygiene culture through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-005, and cybersecurity protocols through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-006.

practices.name: Provide incident management training
practices.description: Organizations should adopt incident management training as part of cybersecurity measures for coordinating and managing incidents.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports incident management training through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-006, which recommends incident management training, educational booklets, and cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing incidents.

practices.name: Use cybersecurity protocols for incident coordination
practices.description: Organizations should adopt cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing cybersecurity incidents.
practices.support_notes: Karayel supports cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing incidents through EVID-KARAYEL-ORG-TRAINING-POLICIES-006.

practices.name: Use a VPN on free or public Wi-Fi
practices.description: Users should use a VPN when using free or public Wi-Fi.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports VPN use on free/public Wi-Fi through EVID-VISHWANATH-TRANSMISSION-002-001. Baraković and Baraković Husić list VPN use on public Wi-Fi as good transmission/browsing hygiene through EVID-BARAKOVIC-TRANSMISSION-002. Do not claim VPN use makes public Wi-Fi completely safe.

practices.name: Store logins and passwords in an encrypted online password vault
practices.description: Users should store logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports storing logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults through EVID-VISHWANATH-TRANSMISSION-002-002. The credential-targeting mitigation is a reasonable mapping, but the evidence directly supports the source-listed CHI practice rather than proving a specific threat-reduction mechanism.

practices.name: Set online alerts for personal information
practices.description: Users should place online alerts for their name or personal information to monitor possible exposure of personal information online.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports placing online alerts for one's name or personal information through EVID-VISHWANATH-TRANSMISSION-002-003. The privacy-exposure mitigation should be interpreted as monitoring or detecting possible exposure, not as preventing oversharing or identity theft.

practices.name: Create new and unique logins and passwords
practices.description: Users should create new and unique logins and passwords for each account and avoid using the same password across multiple accounts.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports creating new/unique logins and passwords through EVID-VISHWANATH-AUTH-005. Cain supports not using passwords for multiple accounts through EVID-CAIN-PASSWORD-HYGIENE-004 and describes developing unique passwords as ideal good cyber hygiene through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-001. The unauthorized-access mitigation is reasonable but partly inferred.

practices.name: Manage browser-stored passwords
practices.description: Users should manage passwords stored by browsers as part of authentication and credential hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports managing browser-stored passwords through EVID-VISHWANATH-AUTH-004. Baraković and Baraković Husić describe authentication and credential hygiene as including managing how browsers store passwords through EVID-BARAKOVIC-AUTH-001. The unauthorized-access mitigation is reasonable but partly inferred.

practices.name: Know social media connections
practices.description: Users should be aware of who they are connected to on social media.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports knowing social media connections through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-002. Baraković and Baraković Husić list knowing social media connections as a social media hygiene example through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. Privacy exposure is the strongest mitigation mapping; oversharing should only be added if framed as limiting audience exposure.

practices.name: Reassess social media friends and connections
practices.description: Users should periodically reassess their social media friends and connections.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports reassessing social media friends/connections through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić list reassessing social media friends/connections through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. Privacy exposure and impersonation are reasonable mappings; oversharing is weaker unless framed as limiting exposure to inappropriate audiences.

practices.name: Prevent location leaks in social media posts
practices.description: Users should ensure that their location information is not leaked in social media posts.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports preventing location leakage in posts through EVID-VISHWANATH-SOCIAL-MEDIA-005. Baraković and Baraković Husić list ensuring location information is not leaked in posts through EVID-BARAKOVIC-SOCIAL-MEDIA-001. Privacy exposure and oversharing are appropriate mappings because location leakage is a specific form of personal-information exposure.

practices.name: Enable firewalls
practices.description: Users should enable firewalls on computing devices as part of storage and device hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports enabling firewalls through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-003. Cain supports firewalls as secure-computing factors, government-listed guidance, and user firewall behavior through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-001, EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-003, and EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-006. The unauthorized-access mitigation is reasonable but partly inferred.

practices.name: Check device operating system and software updates
practices.description: Users should check whether their device has the latest operating system, software update, or patch, and should not delay security-related software patches or upgrades.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports checking OS/software/patch status through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-004. Cain supports updating applications/software/operating systems through EVID-CAIN-STORAGE-DEVICE-003. Baraković and Baraković Husić support latest operating systems and updated software through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-001. Oz supports the ransomware patching context through EVID-OZ-RANSOMWARE-PATCHING-001. Do not claim patching eliminates all ransomware risk.

practices.name: Monitor device resource and network usage
practices.description: Users should monitor device processes such as CPU, power, and network usage as part of storage and device hygiene.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports monitoring CPU, power, or network usage through EVID-VISHWANATH-STORAGE-DEVICE-005. The malware-infection mitigation is a cautious device-hygiene mapping; the evidence supports monitoring behavior itself rather than proving it detects malware.

practices.name: Check email hyperlinks and attachments from unknown senders
practices.description: Users should be cautious with hyperlinks and attachments in emails from unknown senders before clicking or opening them, especially because phishing and ransomware may use malicious links or attachments.
practices.support_notes: Vishwanath supports the CHI item concerning hyperlinks or attachments in email from unknown senders through EVID-VISHWANATH-EMAIL-MESSAGING-004. Cain supports cautious handling of emails from unknown sources, phishing consequences, warning signs, and unsafe user behavior through EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-001, EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-002, EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-003, and EVID-CAIN-EMAIL-MESSAGING-004. NIST supports not clicking suspicious email attachments or hyperlinks through EVID-NIST-PHISHING-SOCIAL-ENGINEERING-001. Oz supports the ransomware infection-vector context through EVID-OZ-RANSOMWARE-INFECTION-VECTORS-001.

practices.name: Limit sharing personal information online
practices.description: Users should limit sharing private or personal information online, especially on social networks, including information such as real names, dates of birth, addresses, phone numbers, or email addresses where disclosure is unnecessary.
practices.support_notes: Cain supports limiting private/personal information sharing through EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-002, which identifies sharing private information over social networks as poor cyber hygiene; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-004, which states that personal information can be stolen from social networking sites; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-005, which links personal information to social engineering such as spear phishing; EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-006 and EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-007, which report disclosure behaviors; and EVID-CAIN-POOR-HYGIENE-008, which states that many users share too much personal information.

practices.name: Password protect mobile devices
practices.description: Users should protect mobile devices with authentication, such as a PIN or password, but this practice requires a direct evidence object before being treated as source-supported in this KB.
practices.support_notes: No direct EVID object was found in the current KU evidence map for password- or PIN-protecting mobile devices. Do not use SRC-CAIN-2018 for this practice unless a concrete EVID-CAIN-* object is added. The mitigation links are conceptually reasonable but should be treated as unsupported until direct evidence exists.

practices.name: Secure wireless networks
practices.description: Users should secure their wireless networks, including password-protecting Wi-Fi, but this exact practice requires a direct evidence object before being treated as source-supported in this KB.
practices.support_notes: No direct EVID object was found in the current KU evidence map for securing personal wireless networks or password-protecting Wi-Fi. Karayel and Baraković support caution around unknown, insecure, public, or password-protected Wi-Fi networks, but that is not the same as direct support for this practice. Add a concrete EVID object before using this PRA as a supported recommendation.

practices.name: Use checked and safe download links
practices.description: Users should prefer checked and safe links when downloading software or applications on computers or smartphones, but the current evidence supports this mainly as a behavior/concern area rather than a detailed standalone practice.
practices.support_notes: Baraković and Baraković Husić support the download-link behavior/concern area through EVID-BARAKOVIC-STORAGE-DEVICE-BEHAVIOR-004, which reports concerning results for links used for software and application downloads on computers and smartphones. This supports the practice directionally, but the exact wording 'use checked and safe download links' should be treated as a conservative practical rewording. The unpatched-software mitigation was removed because safe download links do not directly address patching.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene as routine security behavior for protecting personal information
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene refers to routine cybersecurity practices that online users adopt to protect the security, safety, and integrity of their personal information on internet-connected devices against cyber threats.
knowledge_units.explanation: Cyber hygiene consists of simple, routine cybersecurity practices that help online users protect personal information on internet-connected or internet-enabled devices and reduce exposure to cyber threats.

recommended_actions.action_text: Adopt routine cybersecurity practices when using internet-connected devices.
Protect the security, safety, and integrity of personal information on internet-connected devices.
Use simple, routine security measures to reduce cyber threat risk.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Cyber hygiene refers to a set of cybersecurity practices that online users should adopt to safeguard the security and integrity of their personal information on internet-connected devices against potential cyber threats.
As a fundamental principle of information security, cyber hygiene comprises simple, routine measures that mitigate the risk of cyber threats.

the cyber security practices that online consumers should engage in to protect the safety and integrity of their personal information on their Internet enabled devices from being compromised in a cyber-attack

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene guidance should address user behavior as well as technical controls
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene guidance should address individual user behavior as well as technical cybersecurity measures, because cybersecurity investments and measures alone are insufficient. Good cyber hygiene involves following security best practices and protecting personal information.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU explains why cyber hygiene guidance should not be treated as only a technical-control issue. Karayel et al. identify cybersecurity investments and measures alone as insufficient, describe human factors as a weak link, and mention noncompliance with security practices as a human-factor concern. Cain et al. add that individuals with good cyber hygiene follow security best practices and protect their personal information, while users may lack understanding of necessary cybersecurity actions.

recommended_actions.action_text: Address individual user behavior when designing cyber hygiene guidance.
Do not rely only on technical cybersecurity investments or measures when explaining cyber hygiene.
Treat noncompliance with security practices as a cyber hygiene concern.
Encourage individuals to follow security best practices that protect their personal information.
Explain necessary cybersecurity actions clearly so users understand what protective behavior involves.

noncompliance with security practices

Claims about technical measures being insufficient, human factors, and noncompliance should be traceable to Karayel et al.'s Introduction. Claims about good cyber hygiene involving security best practices, personal information protection, or users lacking understanding of necessary cybersecurity actions should be traceable to Cain et al.'s Abstract or Statement of the problem section. Baraković and Baraković Husić may be used only as contextual support for cybersecurity behavior and human-behavior-related device outcomes.

knowledge_units.title: Authentication hygiene includes changing defaults, creating complex or unique credentials, managing browser-stored passwords, and enabling MFA
knowledge_units.claim: Authentication and credential hygiene involves changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex or unique credentials, managing browser-stored passwords, avoiding password reuse as lax credential behavior, and enabling multifactor authentication.
knowledge_units.explanation: These practices describe authentication and credential hygiene behaviors that help users manage access to accounts and systems through usernames, passwords, stored credentials, and authentication mechanisms. Karayel et al. support changing defaults, creating complex credentials, and enabling multifactor authentication. Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes changing default usernames and passwords, creating new and unique logins and passwords, managing browser-stored passwords, and enabling two-factor or multifactor authentication. Baraković and Baraković Husić support the broader authentication and credential hygiene category, including secure browsers, default credential changes, browser-stored password management, password reuse as lax security behavior, brute-force risk from weak passwords, and MFA as an additional authentication factor.

recommended_actions.action_text: Change default usernames and passwords.
Create complex usernames and passwords.
Create new and unique logins and passwords for each account.
Manage passwords stored by browsers.
Enable two-factor or multifactor authentication where possible.
Avoid password reuse as part of authentication and credential hygiene.
Do not treat strong passwords alone as protection against password-stealing attacks such as keylogging and phishing.

question_templates: What are good authentication and credential hygiene practices?
How can users improve password and authentication security?
What should remote workers do to protect their login credentials?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex usernames and passwords and enabling multifactor authentication

High authentication and credential hygiene, which involve using complex usernames and passwords, changing defaults and enabling multifactor authentication, are observed in the IT, defense, automotive and manufacturing industries. In contrast, the food, service and construction industries demonstrate the lowest levels. As user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers, maintaining high cyber hygiene is crucial for all internet users.
Changing default username from administrator to something unique on all Internet enabled devices

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Authentication and credential hygiene describe activities related to use of secure browsers, changing of default username and passwords, managing how browser stores passwords, etc.
It was found that lax security behavior included password reuse, lack of knowledge about the (in)secure passwords, sharing or writing down the passwords.

Furthermore, Florêncio et al. (2007) found out that strong passwords did not protect the online users from password stealing attacks, such as keylogging and phishing. Weak passwords invited brute-force attack.
Moreover, multi-factor authentication has appeared as an alternative practice to enhance security by requesting the user to provide more than one authentication factor contrary to only a password.

Authentication and credential hygiene includes enabling multifactor authentication.

knowledge_units.title: Routine cyber hygiene practices include password, antivirus, storage, and security scanning behaviors
knowledge_units.claim: Routine cyber hygiene practices include checking computers for cyber threats or attack attempts, using strong passwords, changing passwords regularly, avoiding password reuse, updating antivirus software, securely storing online information, and conducting appropriate security scans.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU provides concrete examples of routine cyber hygiene behaviors listed by Karayel et al. These examples cover password behavior, antivirus updates, secure online information storage, and security scanning. The KU should be used as a basic examples node rather than as a complete checklist of all cyber hygiene practices.

recommended_actions.action_text: Check computers for cyber threats or attack attempts.
Use strong passwords.
Change passwords regularly.
Avoid password reuse.
Keep antivirus software up to date.
Securely store online information.
Conduct appropriate security scans.

knowledge_units.title: Email and messaging hygiene includes checking senders, headers, links, attachments, and suspicious message features
knowledge_units.claim: Email and messaging hygiene involves checking incoming email subjects, headers, sender domains, hyperlinks or attachments from unknown senders, and grammatical or typographical errors. knowledge_units.explanation: This KU covers the detection-oriented side of email and messaging hygiene: what users should inspect when evaluating suspicious or unexpected email and messaging requests. knowledge_units.title: Phishing response hygiene includes caution with unknown senders, avoiding unsafe replies or clicks, and understanding risky email behaviors
knowledge_units.claim: Phishing response hygiene involves approaching emails from unknown sources cautiously, avoiding replies to requests for financial or personal information, verifying suspicious requests through legitimate contact channels, and avoiding attachments or hyperlinks in suspicious emails. knowledge_units.explanation: This KU covers the response-oriented side of email and phishing hygiene. Cain et al.'s phishing percentages should only be interpreted as findings from their study sample, not as population-level rates for all users.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Transmission and browsing hygiene is associated with internet connections, since users are unaware of the risks related to connecting to an unknown or unsecure wireless-fidelity (Wi-Fi) network or leaving Wi-Fi turned on the whole time.

Good practices include using virtual private network (VPN) while using a public Wi-Fi network, checking the quality of the secure socket layer (SSL) certificate, and checking the lock icon on the web browser.

Results show that 60.3% of participants use public Wi-Fi networks that are available and free, 49.1% use password protected public Wi-Fi networks, and 37.7% use Wi-Fi networks that are familiar to them.

In terms of security awareness, it is concerning that only half of participants stated that it is not safe to use Wi-Fi networks which require access password for sensitive activities (Q15). The big issue is that almost 41% of participants are not sure.

Namely, two thirds of participants stated that they are not sure (59.3%) whether all Wi-Fi traffic is encrypted by default on all wireless routers (Q16) and that it is (24.4%). Only 16.3% answered correctly, that it is not encrypted.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene guidance needs clear conceptual boundaries
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene guidance needs clear conceptual boundaries because vague, broadly construed, untested, or conflicting advice can confuse users and weaken their ability to protect cyberspace.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU supports the motivation for structured cyber hygiene guidance. Vishwanath et al. argue that cyber hygiene has been widely discussed while remaining conceptually unclear, with limited prior scholarly explication or measurement. They also state that vague, broad, mostly untested, or conflicting advice can confuse IT managers and end-users and weaken cyber resilience. This supports organizing cyber hygiene guidance around clear concepts, defined dimensions, and traceable claims rather than presenting cyber hygiene as an unstructured collection of advice.

knowledge_units.title: Password hygiene includes strength, uniqueness, memorability, regular changes, and not sharing passwords
knowledge_units.claim: Cain et al. summarize prior password-hygiene guidance as selecting strong passwords, avoiding personal information or dictionary words, avoiding reuse across multiple accounts, changing passwords often, not sharing passwords with others, and considering memorability because impractical password rules can lead users to workarounds.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU covers password-hygiene guidance discussed by Cain et al. The reviewed password section identifies weak passwords as a major source of network vulnerability and states that users are advised to select strong passwords to prevent guessing attacks. It also says passwords should not include personal information or dictionary words, should be complex but memorable, should not be used for multiple accounts, should be changed often, and should not be shared with others. Cain et al. also note a usability tension: long, complex alphanumeric passwords may be difficult to remember and can push users toward workarounds. The empirical percentages from Cain et al. should be treated as results from their study sample, not as universal rates for all users.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: The main aim of this paper is to evaluate, analyze, and understand the level of cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices of university students and their mutual relations.
Results show that students have acceptable cyber hygiene behavior, but their awareness is not satisfactory, and their knowledge is quite low.

Also, the study shows the existence of some relations between gender and current education level and cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavior, as well as mutual interplay and relations between those cyber hygiene outcomes.
It is common view that user cyber hygiene can be measured by three user-oriented factors, i.e., knowledge, awareness, and behavior.
Knowledge and awareness refer to cognitive factors of knowledge and understanding, respectively, whereas behavioral practices are related to factor of use.
Good cyber hygiene is based on these three factors: when educated users are aware of what must be done, they implement it when necessary.

Tables 5–7 show the summaries of Chi-square test results related to relations of gender and current education level and cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavior organized by cyber hygiene dimensions.
Tables 8–10 cover results related to mutual interplay and relations between cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices obtained by conducting Chi-square test as well.

Research results show that university students in Bosnia and Herzegovina have relatively acceptable cyber hygiene behavior, but their awareness of cyber hygiene is not satisfactory, as well as their knowledge, which is quite low.

The results of Chi-square test are given in Table 8. The results show the statistically significant association between computer sharing behavioral practices and awareness to enable security software on those devices and to use safe links on smartphones. Smartphone usage habits have statistically significant relation to awareness of smartphone software updating. Wi-Fi usage habits have statistically significant association with awareness on using Wi-Fi for mobile banking.

Similar conclusion can be made regarding the relation between cyber hygiene behavioral practices and knowledge. This relation exists in some cases, but it needs to be examined in more detail in a focused study.

Data collected under items Q3, Q6, and Q12 has not been analyzed due to the same reasons stated above. We have analyzed the existence of relation between computer (Q4) and smartphone (Q5) sharing habits and cyber hygiene knowledge items (Q11, Q16-Q19, Q21-26). Then, we have analyzed the relation between password creation and management behavior (Q9 and Q10) and relevant knowledge data (Q11), and Wi-Fi usage data (Q13 and Q14) and relevant knowledge data (Q16).
Similar, after the analysis conducted by using Chi-square test with cyber hygiene awareness and knowledge items, one can conclude that the results indicate the partial existence of the relation.

There are statistically significant associations between awareness on Wi-Fi safety and correct answer on Q16-Q19 and Q23-24. Also, there are statistically significant associations between awareness on device and file checking and correct answer on Q11 and Q22.

knowledge_units.claim: Baraković and Baraković Husić recommend that cyber hygiene programs combine proactive technical and behavioral measures with education and training that improve knowledge, attitudes, awareness, and routine behavior.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU captures Baraković and Baraković Husić's program-level cyber hygiene recommendations. In their conclusion, they recommend using the study to develop a cyber hygiene program and guidelines for organizations and individuals. Their listed proactive measures include limiting hardware or software access, regular hardware and software updates, antivirus or anti-malware tools, complex password management, multifactor authentication, regular backups, avoiding suspicious sites and email attachments, and making cyber hygiene part of routine behavior. They also recommend educational trainings and programs to increase cyber hygiene knowledge, develop attitudes toward cybersecurity threats, convert awareness into behavioral practices, and share good practices. These recommendations should be treated as practical program guidance, not as proof that the measures alone eliminate cyber threats.

In this term, they focus on proactive measures to improve user experience and security, such as: (i) limit user access to hardware/software, (ii) update hardware/software regularly, (iii) use antivirus/anti-malware tools, (iv) manage complex passwords, (v) use multi-factor authentication, (vi) backup regularly, (vii) avoid suspicious sites and e-mail attachments, (viii) make cyber hygiene part of routine.

Furthermore, practical implications refer to development of specific educational trainings and programs with the purpose to: (i) increase the knowledge regarding cyber hygiene concepts and issues, (ii) develop new attitudes toward cybersecurity threats, (iii) convert cybersecurity awareness into behavioral practices, (iv) build new rules sharing good practices for cyber hygiene.
In addition, cyber hygiene programs need to be gender-aware to provide the opportunity for both sexes to gain the same knowledge and awareness levels in terms of cybersecurity.

knowledge_units.title: Ransomware restricts access to systems or data to extort payment
knowledge_units.claim: Ransomware is a subset of malware that prevents or limits access to a victim's system or data until a ransom is paid. Oz et al. classify ransomware into cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts files, and locker ransomware, which blocks access to a system without necessarily encrypting data.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU defines ransomware and explains its main taxonomy and infection-vector context. Oz et al. define ransomware as malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system or data until a ransom is paid. They classify ransomware into cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts victim files and demands payment for decryption, and locker ransomware, which prevents access to a system without necessarily encrypting user data. They also describe ransomware attack phases as infection, possible command-and-control communication, destruction, and extortion, and list infection vectors including malicious emails, SMS or instant messages, malicious applications, drive-by downloads, and vulnerabilities. This KU should be used for ransomware definition, taxonomy, phases, and infection-vector awareness, not as direct support for backup or recovery guidance.

recommended_actions.action_text: Treat ransomware as a malware threat that can prevent or limit access to systems or data until a ransom is paid.
Distinguish cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts victim files, from locker ransomware, which locks access to a system without necessarily encrypting user data.
Explain ransomware attacks through phases such as infection, possible command-and-control communication, destruction, and extortion.
Be cautious with ransomware infection vectors such as malicious emails, SMS or instant messages, malicious applications, drive-by downloads, and vulnerabilities.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Ransomware is a subset of malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system and/or data until a ransom is paid. The main objective of ransomware is extorting money from the victims.

Based on the employed methodology, ransomware is generally classified into two types. Cryptographic Ransomware: This variety of ransomware encrypts victim files, deletes or overwrites the original files, and demands a ransom payment for decryption of the files. Locker Ransomware: This type of ransomware prevents the victim from accessing its system by locking the screen or browser, and demands a ransom payment to unlock the system.  Unlike cryptographic ransomware, it does not encrypt the system or user data
Attack phases of ransomware can be summarized as follows: Infection... Communication with C&C servers... Destruction... Extortion.

Infection methods of ransomware can be categorized into five groups: malicious e-mails, SMS or instant messages (IMs), malicious applications, drive-by-download, and vulnerabilities. Malicious e-mails are the most commonly used infection vectors for ransomware. Attackers send spam e-mails to victims that have attachments containing ransomware [164]. Such spam campaigns can be distributed using botnets [110, 139]. Ransomware may come with an attached malicious file, or the e-mail may contain a malicious link that will trigger the installation of ransomware once visited (drive-by download). SMS Messages or IMs are used frequently for mobile ransomware. In such kinds of infections, attackers send SMS messages or IMs to the victims that will cause them to browse a malicious website to download ransomware to their platforms [140, 147]. Malicious Applications are used by ransomware attackers who develop and deploy mobile applications that contain ransomware camouflaged as a benign application [140, 147]. Drive-by download happens when a user unknowingly visits an infected website or clicks a malicious advertisement (i.e., malvertisement) and then the malware is downloaded and installed without the user’s knowledge [176]. Vulnerabilities in the victim platform such as vulnerabilities in operating systems [40], browsers [163], or software can be used by ransomware authors as infection vectors. Attackers can use helper applications, exploit kits, to exploit the known or zero-day vulnerabilities in target systems. Attackers can redirect victims to those kits via malvertisement and malicious links

knowledge_units.title: Ransomware defense includes recovery mechanisms, timely patching, and training about infection vectors
knowledge_units.claim: Oz et al. describe ransomware defense and recovery as involving recovery mechanisms such as key recovery, hardware-based file recovery, cloud backup-based recovery, and mobile-device recovery approaches, while also emphasizing timely upgrades or security-related software patches and end-user training about ransomware infection vectors.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU covers ransomware recovery and selected defense-related recommendations from Oz et al. For PCs and workstations, Oz et al. describe ransomware recovery as involving key recovery, hardware-based file recovery, or cloud backup-based file recovery. For mobile devices, they discuss recovery approaches involving flash-memory behavior analysis and periodic backups to external storage. Oz et al. also state that it is vital not to delay upgrades or security-related software patches to prevent infection through vulnerabilities, and that end-user security training about ransomware infection vectors is very vital. This supports treating ransomware defense as a combination of recovery planning, timely patching, and user training, while avoiding claims that backups, patching, training, or detection mechanisms alone guarantee protection or recovery.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Ransomware recovery research for PCs/workstations shows that recovery of the destruction performed by ransomware can be achieved in three different ways: recovery of keys, recovery of files via hardware, or recovery of files via cloud backup.
Recovery of Keys: Kolodenker et al. proposed PayBreak - a key-escrow mechanism that intends to capture encryption key(s) by hooking the cryptography APIs and decrypt the victim files.

Recovery of Files via Hardware: The studies presented in this category aim to recover encrypted files of victims by utilizing the characteristics of storage hardware (i.e., SSD).  Alternatively... Min et al. designed an SSD system that performs an automated backup and minimizes the backup space overhead. Their system utilizes a detection component that leverages hardware accelerator to detect the infected pages in the memory.

Recovery of Files via Cloud Backup: Some of the recovery mechanisms in the literature aimed to recover files utilizing cloud environment for backup purposes. Yun et al. proposed a backup system named CLDSafe that is deployed on the cloud.

Ransomware Recovery for Mobile Devices. Considering the recovery solutions for mobile devices to enable data recovery from ransomware attacks, there exist only two studies... a recovery-based ransomware defense strategy for mobile devices that are equipped with flash memory as external storage. It collects the access behaviors of ransomware samples and applies K-mean clustering to identify the unique access patterns to the Flash Transaction Layer. In [59] Yalew et al. aimed to recover from ransomware by periodically performing backups to an external storage.
Although upgrades and patches may aim to repair such vulnerabilities, it is vital not to delay upgrades or security-related software patches to prevent the infection.
Security training of end-users in terms of the infection vectors of ransomware is very vital.

knowledge_units.title: Malware is malicious code that can compromise data, applications, or operating systems
knowledge_units.claim: NIST defines malware, also known as malicious code, as covertly inserted software intended to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of data, applications, or operating systems.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU provides a definition-focused NIST malware node. NIST defines malware, also known as malicious code, as a covertly inserted program intended to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of a victim's data, applications, or operating system. NIST also lists classic malware categories including viruses, worms, Trojan horses, malicious mobile code, and blended attacks, and defines Trojan horses as self-contained, nonreplicating programs that appear benign while having a hidden malicious purpose. NIST further notes that modern malware often combines characteristics, making classic categories less useful for malware incident handling than they once were. This KU should be used for malware definition and forms, not as direct support for specific prevention practices such as backups, scans, updates, or email-link checking.

knowledge_units.title: Malware prevention requires layered controls, not a single defensive tool
knowledge_units.claim: NIST frames malware prevention as a layered effort involving policy, awareness, vulnerability mitigation, threat mitigation, and defensive architecture, while noting that malware incidents can still occur despite prevention efforts.
knowledge_units.explanation: This KU explains NIST's layered approach to malware prevention. NIST identifies policy, awareness, vulnerability mitigation, threat mitigation, and defensive architecture as the main prevention elements. NIST also warns that malware incidents will still occur despite prevention efforts, including because of previously unknown threats and human error. The awareness guidance includes avoiding suspicious emails, attachments, hyperlinks, suspicious popups, files commonly associated with malware, untrusted applications, disabled malware security controls, and administrator-level accounts for regular host operation.
